title: Solanum trinominum
taxonomy: Solanum trinominum is a species of flowering plant in the nightshade family Solanaceae. It is a perennial herb with a distinctive growth habit and distribution. As a member of the Solanaceae family, it is closely related to other plants such as tomatoes, potatoes, and eggplants. Solanum trinominum is further classified within the tribe Solaneae, which includes various species of nightshade and their relatives. Further taxonomic classification may include the genus Solanum and the subgenus Leptostemonum.

side_effects: Solanum trinominum is a plant species that, like some of its relatives, may potentially have toxic effects due to the presence of solanine and other glycoalkaloids. While it's often used in traditional medicine, improper handling or consumption can cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Ingestion of large amounts can also lead to more severe symptoms such as headaches and abdominal pain. Additionally, touching the plant's leaves or sap may cause skin irritation in some individuals. It's essential to exercise caution and consult with a medical professional before using Solanum trinominum for medicinal purposes.
intro: Solanum trinominum, also known as Three-Named Nightshade, is a plant species within the Solanaceae family, characterized by its unique trinominal naming convention and potential medicinal properties. The plant has been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including inflammation, fever, and respiratory problems, due to its antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and expectorant properties. The bioactive constituents of Solanum trinominum include solasodine glycosides, alkaloids, and other compounds that contribute to its medicinal effects. Herbal preparations made from Solanum trinominum include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to alleviate symptoms associated with colds, flu, and other upper respiratory tract infections.
